frictions.text: n007 (guidelines dropped the cholesterol cap) is offered as a Ground for 'eggs are ~neutral', but it is an APPEAL TO AUTHORITY / consensus signal, not direct biological or epidemiological evidence. The grammar has no way to mark that this support is evidential-weak / rhetorical rather than a first-order reason. FLF explicitly wants rhetorical-vs-evidential moves surfaced; a plain Ground edge hides the distinction.

A SINGLE source (eggs-001, Hu 1999) yields TWO findings that support DIFFERENT positions: no CVD link in non-diabetics (grounds N1) and higher risk in diabetics (grounds N3). I had to split it into two external_anchor nodes both citing eggs-001. Nothing records that these are the same study, so a reader can't see that one dataset cuts both ways, and double-counting its evidential weight is invisible.

n008 (yolks ~185mg cholesterol) is a hard empirical fact that should exit the graph to a food-composition database, but the source pack has no such reference, so I left it as a bare internal claim. The grammar's 'external_anchor must cite a pack ref' rule and the pack's coverage are out of sync; the graph looks internally-grounded where it should touch reality.

I placed n005 (diet cholesterol weak effect for most) and n009 (raises LDL, esp. hyper-responders) in one antithesis set as 'in tension', but they are NOT mutually exclusive — both can be true at once (weak on average, strong in a subgroup). Antithesis membership renders them as rivals of equal kind to the N1/N2/N3 answers, overstating the opposition. Need graded / partial tension, not binary set membership.

n017 (confounding) does not deny n013 (the diabetic association is real); it attacks the INFERENCE from n013 to a causal reading. Support-only has no undercut edge, so I was forced to invent a positive 'it's causal' node (n016) purely to have something for the confounding claim to be antithesis to. This distorts an argument about inference-strength into a rival factual claim. This is the core relational-sufficiency finding: undercutting defeaters do not fit cleanly.

n010 ('elevated LDL causally raises CVD') is a huge separately-contested question (the diet-heart hypothesis) that the egg argument LEANS ON but truncates at a single external anchor (n011). The boundary where we stop decomposing is doing load-bearing work and is semi-arbitrary — the relevance-horizon problem. A reader can't tell whether we endorse n010 or merely need it.

n015 ('high-risk people SHOULD limit eggs') is normative but is grounded by purely empirical claims (n003/n013). The grammar marks no is->ought transition, so a value-laden conclusion and a factual ground look identical as nodes and the Ground edge silently crosses the is/ought gap.

F8: n019 ('observational data can't show causation') is a GENERAL methodological principle that bears on every causal inference in the graph, but I could only attach it as a local Ground of one node (n017). It wants to be a single reusable node linked to many inference points (a shared/wormhole node), not re-authored per site; as a lone local ground it understates its scope.

F9: n021 (the association is real/replicated) is COMMON GROUND both rivals accept; the real disagreement (crux) is only the causal-vs-confounded interpretation. The grammar marks no difference between 'premise both sides share' and 'the crux where they diverge' -- both are just grounds/antitheses. FLF explicitly wants cruxes surfaced; worth a future affordance.

phrasings.text: For most healthy adults, moderate egg consumption (about one per day) has little or no effect on cardiovascular-disease risk.
For the general adult population, eating an egg a day is not linked to higher heart-disease risk.
Regular egg consumption meaningfully increases cardiovascular-disease risk for many people.
The health effect of eggs depends strongly on individual factors, notably diabetes status and cholesterol hyper-responsiveness, rather than being uniform across people.
In large U.S. prospective cohorts, consumption of up to about one egg per day showed no significant association with cardiovascular disease among people without diabetes.
Dietary cholesterol has only a weak effect on serum LDL for most people, because the body compensates by reducing its own cholesterol production.
When dietary cholesterol intake rises, the liver downregulates its own cholesterol synthesis (feedback regulation).
Later editions of the U.S. Dietary Guidelines removed the explicit numeric daily cap on dietary cholesterol.
Egg yolks are high in dietary cholesterol, roughly 185 mg per large egg.
Dietary cholesterol raises serum LDL cholesterol, substantially so in a subset of people known as hyper-responders.
Elevated serum LDL cholesterol is a causal driver of cardiovascular disease.
Foundational multi-country epidemiology correlated dietary fat patterns with serum cholesterol levels and coronary heart disease rates.
Because yolks are cholesterol-rich and dietary cholesterol raises serum LDL, regular egg consumption raises serum LDL, at least in susceptible people.
In the same cohorts, among people with diabetes, higher egg consumption was associated with increased coronary heart disease risk.
A subset of people are cholesterol hyper-responders whose serum LDL rises markedly in response to dietary cholesterol.
People at elevated cardiovascular risk, such as those with type 2 diabetes, should moderate their egg intake.
The elevated cardiovascular risk seen in diabetics with high egg intake reflects a causal effect of eggs.
The elevated cardiovascular risk seen in diabetics with high egg intake may reflect confounding by overall diet and lifestyle rather than a causal effect of eggs.
People with type 2 diabetes tend to have poorer overall dietary patterns and more clustered cardiovascular risk factors than non-diabetics, independent of egg intake.
Observational cohort associations cannot by themselves establish that eggs causally raise cardiovascular risk; residual confounding always remains possible.
When analyses adjust for overall diet quality and lifestyle, observed egg-cardiovascular associations tend to weaken.
The higher egg-coronary-heart-disease association among people with diabetes is real and has appeared across multiple independent cohorts.
Diabetic dyslipidemia can amplify the LDL response to dietary cholesterol, giving a plausible biological mechanism for greater egg-related risk specifically in diabetics.
A dose-response meta-analysis found no association between egg consumption and coronary heart disease in the general population.
The same meta-analysis reported higher coronary-heart-disease risk with egg consumption among people with diabetes.
Mendelian randomization studies find genetically lower lifelong LDL tracks with lower cardiovascular risk, supporting a causal LDL-to-CVD link.
Randomized trials of LDL-lowering therapy reduce cardiovascular events, further supporting causality.
For people at elevated cardiovascular risk, avoiding a modest, low-cost dietary risk factor is worthwhile even when the expected benefit is uncertain.
